invariant_definition: As an epistemic term, *śabda* is verbal testimony that yields valid cognition. It is not merely 'language' in the abstract, nor merely scriptural quotation. The category covers the cognition produced by a meaningful utterance under the right conditions, and therefore sits amid wider disputes about sentence meaning, speaker intention, reliability, and the authority of the Veda. Nyāya grounds testimonial force in the utterance of a trustworthy speaker; Mīmāṃsā insists that Vedic *śabda* is autonomous and not reducible to any speaker; Buddhist epistemology tries to reduce testimony to inference; Vedānta inherits the Mīmāṃsā defense because *brahman* is known only through *śruti*. In practice this means that *śabda* is not a decorative supplement to perception and inference. It is the decisive *pramāṇa* in every domain where the object is not perceptually available and not inferable from worldly marks, especially in the interpretation of Upaniṣadic sentences and *mahāvākyas*.

per_school.definition: For Advaita, Upaniṣadic *śabda* is the decisive *pramāṇa* for *brahman* because the *mahāvākya* itself, assisted by *manana* and related disciplines, yields *aparokṣa-jñāna* rather than a merely mediate report [[PV p. 103](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/mahāvākyasyāparokṣa-prayojakatvam)]. The sentence does not point to an externally available object; it generates *brahmakāra-saṃvedana* in which *brahman* is disclosed as immediately manifest [[PV p. 103b](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 103b); [UpS I.17.8](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.8)]. Accordingly, the Vedānta-*mahāvākya* is treated as the *pramāpaka* of *ātmatattva*, with sentence-meaning itself carrying the liberating force [[SB Upodghāta 7](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/7)].

In Viśiṣṭādvaita, *brahmaśabda* primarily denotes Puruṣottama, whose svarūpa is free from all defect and constituted by ananta-*kalyāṇa-guṇa* [[ŚrīBh 1.1.1 c.6](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/6); [ŚrīBh 1.1.1 c.10](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/10)]. The diverse Vedic terms for the supreme are not loose synonyms but coordinated disclosures of the one *antaryāmin*, and the *śrutis* teach this through *sāmānādhikaraṇya* and correlated word-series such as *śakti*, *aṃśa*, and *śarīra* [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. *Śabda* here is therefore the ordered manifestation of Brahman’s qualified reality, not a prompt toward indeterminate abstraction [[ŚrīBh 1.1.1 c.6](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/6); [VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

Tattva-vāda treats Vedic discourse as carrying determinate authority over both the reality and the *prāmāṇya* of the whole body of verbal expression [[BBsBh 1.1.1.13](cite://madhva/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.1.1.13); [BBsBh 1.1.1.15](cite://madhva/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.1.1.15)]. The school’s reading of *śruti* is governed by real difference: *abheda*-statements are admitted only under *sādṛśya*, *pradhānatva*, or kindred modes of secondary predication, never as *svarūpābhidāna* of identity between *jīva* and Īśvara [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)]. Later Tattva-vāda secures this scriptural authority within a *svataḥ-prāmāṇya* framework, so that even the *apauruṣeya* Veda stands as *pramāṇa* in its own right [[TT Prāmāṇyavāda conclusion](cite://vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/conclusion)].

In Bhedābheda, Upaniṣadic *śabda* discloses Brahman without annulling the articulated manifold of *nāma-rūpa* through which Brahman is expressed [[BṛU 1.4.7](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.7); [BṛU 1.4.10](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.10)]. Sentences such as *ahaṃ brahmāsmi* are thus received together with the discipline of *śrotavyo mantavyo nididhyāsitavyaḥ*, so that scriptural hearing initiates contemplative realization rather than a merely nominal identity-claim [[BṛU 2.4.5, 1.4.15](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/2.4.5, 1.4.15)].
primary_loci: Bhartṛprapañca, Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments on 2.4.5 and 1.4.15

per_school.definition: For Acintya-Bhedābheda, scriptural discourse is the place where *bheda* and *abheda* statements are coordinated, since *abheda-śāstra* becomes intelligible only after contradiction has been removed by Bhagavān’s natural *acintya-śakti* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §42](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42); [Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. This hermeneutic yields an *abhidheya* centered on bhajana, because scripture first establishes the difference between Bhagavān as *māyā-āśraya* and the *jīva* as *māyā-mohita* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)]. *Śabda* therefore culminates in the establishment of Bhagavān as the proper object of *parama-prema* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

Dvaitādvaita receives the *śruti*-corpus as internally non-contradictory when read with Kṛṣṇa as its personal *viṣaya* [[Vedānta-Kaustubha Maṅgalācaraṇa 1](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1)]. The school’s scriptural reasoning takes the *jīva* as Paramātman’s *aṃśa*, neither *atyantabhinna* nor *atyantābhinna*, with *aṃśa* understood through *śruti* as a real *śakti*-relation marked by *asvatantratva* [[VK 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)]. Nimbārka accordingly treats the threefold real as *yathārtha*, its *trirūpatā* being established by *śruti*, *smṛti*, and *sūtra* [[DaŚ 7](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/7)].

Śuddhādvaita locates Brahman-knowledge in Vedic disclosure, since Brahman as *aṃśin* is not established in ordinary experience and is *vedaika-samadhigamya* [[AnuBh 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)]. The same *śāstric* disclosure prepares the *jīva* for Puruṣottama-prāpti, while *bhakti-bhāva* functions as the requisite *sahakāri* in making that attainment effective [[AnuBh 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)]. Vallabha’s verbal theology therefore serves a real Brahman whose manifestation into *kāryākāra* does not compromise divine *avikṛtatva* [[AnuBh 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)].

Sāṃkhya admits three pramāṇas, and the third is *āptavacana* alongside perception and inference [[SK 4](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/4)]. Kārikā 5 glosses this third source as *āptaśruti* or *āptavacana*, so verbal cognition enters the system as authoritative transmission rather than as a separate theory of linguistic manifestation [[SK 5](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/5)]. It is especially required for what remains *parokṣa* and *atīndriya* even after inference, which the text says is to be established from *āptāgama* [[SK 6](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/6)].

Yoga accepts *āgama* as one of the three pramāṇas, and Vyāsa defines it as the hearer’s cognition produced from the word of one who has seen or inferred the matter in question [[YS 1.7](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.7); [YBh 1.7](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.7)]. At the same time, the tradition sharply distinguishes bare verbality from valid disclosure, since *vikalpa* is a cognition that follows verbal knowledge while remaining empty of a corresponding object [[YS 1.9](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.9)]. In higher analysis the yogin must separate the superimposed triad of word, object, and cognition, because ordinary understanding is structured by their mutual adhyāsa [[YS 3.17](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.17); [YBh 3.17](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/3.17)].

Nyāya defines *śabda* as *āptopadeśa*, a distinct pramāṇa whose scope extends to both seen and unseen objects [[NS 1.1.7](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.7); [NS 1.1.8](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.8)]. The cognition produced by testimony arises from the efficacy of a trustworthy utterance, and later compendia make this explicit by defining the *āpta* as a *yathārthavaktā* and the relevant verbal unit as a meaningful sentence [[NS 2.1.53](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/2.1.53); [TSg 59](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/59)]. In the Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika synthesis, *śabda* also names a quality apprehended by the ear and residing only in ākāśa, so the epistemic and ontological senses are kept distinct but coordinated [[TSg 33](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/33)].

Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā locates dharma in *śabda* alone, so non-verbal evidence is not to be expected in that domain [[MS 1.3.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.3.1)]. It accordingly treats the relation between word and meaning as *autpattika* and holds that, when the relevant object is otherwise unavailable, that verbal upadeśa is itself *pramāṇa* precisely because it is *anapekṣa* [[MS 1.1.5](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.5)]. *Śabda* here is therefore not a speaker-dependent report among other reports, but the irreducible disclosure of *codanā* and dharma [[MS 1.1.5](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.5); [MS 1.3.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.3.1)].

Mādhyamaka situates *śabda* wholly within *saṃvṛti*, since the Buddha’s teaching proceeds by the two truths and even emptiness itself is a dependent *prajñapti* rather than a self-grounding referent [[MMK 24.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8); [MMK 24.18](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18)]. Verbal cancellation is therefore never an intrinsic metaphysical power of words; Nāgārjuna rejects the attempt to treat one word’s blocking of another as though that settled ontological status [[VV 25](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/25)]. Candrakīrti preserves this conventional efficacy without reification: hearing a word can occasion bodhisattva joy and resolve without conferring *svabhāva* on what is named [[MAv 1.14](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.14)].

Yogācāra does not admit *śabda* as an autonomous *pramāṇa*; word-conditioned cognition arises only in dependence on linguistic and auxiliary conditions, and its field is that of conceptual construction rather than independently given external objects [[PV 2.2](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.2)]. Scriptural discourse is therefore treated as *ābhiprāyika*: the Buddha teaches sense-spheres and related items for the sake of specific disciples without thereby reifying an external object-domain [[Vvs 6-8](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/Karma, Vāsanā, and Scriptural Objection)].

In Trika, *śabda* is grounded in consciousness itself: once *saṃvid* is understood as *vimarśātmikā* and *vimarśa* as *śabdanātmaka*, scripture is the reflexive articulation of awareness rather than an external report [[TSāra 21.3-4](cite://abhinavagupta/tantrasara/21.3-4)]. Accordingly, *āgama* is authoritative because the whole *śāstrasaṃdarbha* is non-different from Parameśvara and discloses a single telos through graded forms of revelation ordered by *adhikāra* [[TSāra 21.3-4](cite://abhinavagupta/tantrasara/21.3-4)].

Jainism does not ordinarily isolate *śabda* as a separate *pramāṇa*; its functional analogue is *śruta-jñāna* within the canonical taxonomy of knowledges [[TS 1.9](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9); [TS 1.20](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.20)]. The Tattvārtha-Sūtra classifies *mati* and *śruta* as the first two, and therefore indirect, forms of cognition [[TS 1.9](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9); [TS 1.11](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.11)]. *Śruta* is explicitly said to be *matipūrva*, so verbal or scriptural cognition depends upon prior cognitive uptake rather than arising as an autonomous *pramāṇa* detached from *mati* [[TS 1.20](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.20)].

translator_note: Lazy glosses to avoid: 'word', 'language', and even unqualified 'testimony'. 'Word' misses the epistemic function; 'language' invites a modern linguistic abstraction; 'testimony' can import a courtroom model of evidence. In these traditions *śabda* names a specific kind of valid cognition-producing utterance, and in Vedānta it carries the full burden of disclosing *brahman*.
